topics.title: Cell Structure
topics.summary:
Cell Structure
Learning objectives
● Identify the main organelles found in eukaryotic cells and state their functions. ● Compare prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.
definitions:
Explanation
All living organisms are composed of cells, and their internal organisation determines what a cell
can do.

The nucleus is enclosed by a double membrane and holds the cell's chromosomal
DNA; this DNA contains genes whose instructions influence the cell's activities.

Mitochondria,
also double-membraned, carry out many of the reactions involved in aerobic respiration and use
energy released from glucose to produce ATP, though this is only part of the overall respiration
process.

● Mitochondria carry out many reactions of aerobic respiration and use energy released
from glucose to produce ATP, but are not the sole site of respiration.
learning_objectives: Understand: Cell Structure Learning objectives ● Identify the main organelles found in eukaryotic cell
Understand: ● Compare prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.
Understand: ● Recognise structural features unique to plant cells.

topics.title: Cellular Respiration
topics.summary:
Cellular Respiration
Learning objectives
● Describe the overall purpose and general equation of aerobic respiration. ● State where glycolysis and the later stages of aerobic respiration take place.
definitions:
Explanation
Building on the organelles introduced in the previous section, respiration is the process by which
cells break down glucose to release energy.

If oxygen is available, the products of
glycolysis, including pyruvate, can enter the mitochondria, where the later stages of aerobic
respiration occur, releasing far more energy overall.

Aerobic respiration depends on a
continuous oxygen supply to proceed, although oxygen is used directly only at a specific point
within these later stages rather than throughout every reaction.

When oxygen is limited or
absent, cells rely instead on anaerobic pathways.
learning_objectives: Understand: Cellular Respiration Learning objectives ● Describe the overall purpose and general equati
Understand: ● State where glycolysis and the later stages of aerobic respiration take place.
Understand: ● Compare aerobic respiration with anaerobic pathways.

topics.title: Energy Transfer in Cells
topics.summary:
Energy Transfer in Cells
Learning objectives
● Describe the roles of ATP and ADP in cells. ● Explain how ATP hydrolysis releases energy for cellular use.
definitions: ● Explain why some energy is always lost as heat during these transfers.

When a cell requires energy, ATP is
hydrolysed back into ADP and phosphate; overall, this hydrolysis reaction releases usable
energy, which the cell can then couple to other processes.

Because ATP is broken down and reformed
continuously rather than stockpiled, it functions as an energy transfer molecule rather than a
long-term energy store; longer-term energy stores include glycogen and lipids.

Key points
● ATP is formed by adding a phosphate group to ADP, using energy released during
respiration.
learning_objectives: Understand: Energy Transfer in Cells Learning objectives ● Describe the roles of ATP and ADP in cells.
Understand: ● Explain how ATP hydrolysis releases energy for cellular use.
Understand: ● Identify processes that rely on ATP as an energy transfer molecule.

topics.title: DNA Structure
topics.summary:
DNA Structure
Learning objectives
● Describe the structure of a DNA nucleotide and the double helix. ● Explain complementary base pairing and its role in accurate replication.
definitions:
Explanation
DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is the molecule that carries genetic information in almost all living
organisms.

This predictable pairing means that each existing strand can act as a template for
building a new complementary strand, allowing the DNA sequence to be copied accurately
when cells divide.

A chromosome consists of a long DNA molecule associated with proteins; in
eukaryotic cells, it becomes highly condensed during cell division, and a gene is a section of
DNA containing instructions for producing a functional product, such as a protein or an RNA
molecule.

● A chromosome consists of a long DNA molecule associated with proteins; a gene is a
section of DNA containing instructions for a functional product.
learning_objectives: Understand: DNA Structure Learning objectives ● Describe the structure of a DNA nucleotide and the dou
Understand: ● Explain complementary base pairing and its role in accurate replication.
Understand: ● Distinguish between DNA, genes, and chromosomes.

topics.title: Gene Expression
topics.summary:
Gene Expression
Learning objectives
● Describe transcription and translation, and state where each occurs. ● Explain the roles of mRNA, ribosomes, codons, and amino acids.
definitions:
Explanation
The sections of DNA introduced in the previous section only have an effect on a cell once their
information is put to use, through a process called gene expression.

The first, transcription, takes place in the nucleus, where the DNA sequence of a gene
is used as a template to build a single strand of messenger RNA (mRNA).

As the ribosome moves along the mRNA, amino acids
are joined together in the order specified, building a protein.

Many genes are expressed in this
way to produce proteins, but not all of them are: some genes are transcribed into RNA
molecules that carry out a function directly, without ever being translated into protein.
learning_objectives: Understand: Gene Expression Learning objectives ● Describe transcription and translation, and state wh
Understand: ● Explain the roles of mRNA, ribosomes, codons, and amino acids.
Understand: ● Recognise that genes can produce proteins or functional RNA molecules.

topics.title: Genetic Inheritance
topics.summary:
Genetic Inheritance
Learning objectives
● Define allele, genotype, and phenotype. ● Explain dominant and recessive inheritance using a Punnett-square example.
definitions:
Most human
body cells are diploid, meaning they contain two sets of chromosomes, one inherited from each
parent.

Gametes
(sperm and egg cells), by contrast, are haploid, containing only one copy of each gene, so that
when they combine at fertilisation, the resulting offspring receives the usual two copies.

An
organism's genotype is the specific combination of alleles it carries for a gene, while its
phenotype is the observable characteristic produced by the interaction between its genotype
and the environment.

In simple cases of complete dominance, a dominant allele affects the
phenotype when one or two copies are present, while the recessive phenotype appears only
when two recessive alleles are inherited.
learning_objectives: Understand: Genetic Inheritance Learning objectives ● Define allele, genotype, and phenotype.
Understand: ● Explain dominant and recessive inheritance using a Punnett-square example.
Understand: ● Distinguish diploid body cells from haploid gametes.
example_applications: Understand: ● Explain dominant and recessive inheritance using a Punnett-square example.

topics.title: Historical Biology Experiments
topics.summary:
Historical Biology Experiments
Learning objectives
● Describe Robert Hooke's observations of cork and their significance. ● Explain what Mendel's pea-plant experiments revealed about inheritance.
learning_objectives: Understand: Historical Biology Experiments Learning objectives ● Describe Robert Hooke's observations
Understand: ● Explain what Mendel's pea-plant experiments revealed about inheritance.
Understand: ● Describe how the Hershey–Chase experiment identified DNA as genetic material.
